reply_text:
Agreed, this is how it was supposed to be interpreted. But to be fair, the problem with this "Gi-hun sacrifices himself for the baby" ending is that both In-ho and the audience have seen multiple times throughout S2/S3 that Gi-hun has already given up on life (from playing Russian rulette with the Recruiter in S2Ep1 to literally begging In-ho to end him in S3Ep1). The baby was the only reason that motivated him to continue on after the rebellion failed. 

Not to mention his daughter, ex-wife and mother are all gone. Gi-hun had nothing waiting for him in the outside world, so why on earth would someone as empathetic and kind-hearted as him want to win the games a second time? I get the show tried to say Gi-hun proved In-ho wrong and the baby symbolizes hope, but are the standards for humanity really so low that it's considered to be a great miracle that an adult is willing to sacrifice themselves for a literal newborn? Besides, not once has In-ho claimed that humans can´t do good things, he himself saved his own brother which was against the rules, but it doesn´t change the fact he sees players as "trash". And based on their meeting in S3, he doesn´t see Gi-hun as "trash" unlike the other players.

Not to mention the show strongly implied the baby may remind In-ho of his deceased wife and unborn child (which is a backstory that was created solely for S2/S3, since in S1 his player file makes no mention of him having a wife), so doesn´t it unintentionally suggest that the antagonist wouldn´t have cared at all without this tragic backstory? Better way to prove In-ho wrong imo would´ve been, if there was no baby plot in S2/S3, if Gi-hun sacrificed himself for Myung-gi, and he did it even if Myung-gi didn´t succeed at being a perfect person during these cruel games.

video_title: Squid Game: Season 3 | Official Trailer | Netflix
reply_text:
Stop yapping. Stop insisting the ending was good. There’s no need to force positive reasons onto a trash finale. Proving humanity exists doesn’t require Gi-hun to throw his life away for a CGI baby. What kind of nonsensical ending is that?

Humans are complex they can be evil, selfish, greedy, compassionate, selfless, cruel, or kind. They make mistakes, they learn, they manipulate, they sacrifice but it all depends on context. The answers are many; human nature cannot be simplified or explained by a single exaggerated act.

This finale ignored all of that. It threw subtlety, logic, and character development out the window. Instead of showing the philosophy of humanity through relationships, choices, and real moral dilemmas, it forced a pointless sacrifice that adds nothing to the story. It’s lazy, it’s illogical, and it’s a disservice to everything the series built. Trash doesn’t even begin to describe it.
